bio2025_3764209,"A. Unity and diversity > A2.3. Viruses (HL only) > A2.3.5. Evidence for several origins of viruses from other organisms","

Discuss the evidence provided by viral structure and function that suggests they evolved from living organisms.Â  Â 

","structured","SFA","
<li>complexity in structure/ big size of some viruses evidence evolution from bacteria that lost cell components/functions
<li>e.g. they might have evolved from a kind of parasitic bacteria that can only replicate inside a host
<li>retroviruses/ some viruses and some eukaryotic cells have similar mechanisms for the synthesis of DNA from RNA/ contain reverse transcriptases
<li>suggesting genetic material moved out of cells and evolved into viruses
<li>they are obligate parasites/, so they could not exist before living cells
<li>all living organisms share same genetic material from universal common ancestor/LUCA, so viruses having similar genetic material must have evolved from cells

bio2025_1238822,"A. Unity and diversity > A1.2. Nucleic acids > A1.2.6. DNA as a double helix made of two antiparallel strands of nucleotides with two strands linked by hydrogen bonding between complementary base pairs","

Describe the structure of the DNA molecule.

","structured","LFA","

a. two stranded/double

b. antiparallel / strands running in opposite directions
OR
one strand organized 5â to 3â and the other 3â to 5â â
c. sugar-phosphate backbone â
d. each strand formed by chains of nucleotides â
e. each nucleotide is formed by a phosphate, a deoxyribose and a base / annotated diagram of a nucleotide clearly indicated as a nucleotide â
f. the bases are adenine, guanine, cytosine and thymine â

g. strands held together by hydrogen bonds (between complementary base pairs)
OR
A pairs with T and C pairs with G â
Both helix and two strands needed for mp a. Double helix is sufficient for the mark.
Points can be awarded to annotated diagrams.
For mp c, the explicit label sugar phosphate backbone is required.
To award mp d from a diagram, at least three pairs of nucleotides should be shown.
For mp e, the diagram would need to be labelled as a nucleotide.
For mp e, expect deoxyribose not just sugar.
The written names of the bases are required for mp f.
Do not penalize twice for mp f and g for using letters.

If they only ever use the symbols A,T,C and G they are ineligible for mp f. If however, they say A pairs with T and C pairs with G, then they would get mp g. If they wrote adenine pairs with thymine and cytosine pairs with guanine, then they would obtain both mp f and mp g.

bio2025_1240766,"A. Unity and diversity > A1.2. Nucleic acids > A1.2.14. Evidence from the HersheyâChase experiment for DNA as the genetic material","

The graph shows results of an experiment by Hershey and Chase in 1952 in which bacteria were infected with a mixture of virus particles labelled with either 32P or 35S. ","structured","LFA","

a. gene pool is all genes/alleles in an (interbreeding)
b. gene pool splits/divides/separated during speciation
c. due to reproductive isolation (of groups within a species)
d. temporal/behavioral/geographic isolation (can cause reproductive isolation)

bio2025_1240215,"A. Unity and diversity > A4.1. Evolution and speciation > A4.1.7. Roles of reproductive isolation and differential selection in speciation","

Explain how speciation occurs, including the different processes of isolation and selection.

","structured","LFA","
<li>Species is a group of organisms that interbreeds (normally in the wild) and produce fertile offspring
<li>within an interbreeding population there is variety / variation exists
<li>some adaptations favour survival to reproductive age /survival of the fittest / natural selection
<li>alleles for these adaptations become more frequent/are inherited in the population /change with time
<li>speciation is the formation of new species
<li>(speciation) occurs because populations have become reproductively isolated / no longer able to interbreed / exposed to different selection pressures
<li>behavioural isolation involves differences in courtship or mating behaviours
<li>temporal isolation involves differences in the timing of courtship or mating behaviours
<li>geographical isolation / allopatric refers to the physical barriers that exist that keep two populations from mating
<li>polyploidy can lead to reproductive isolation
<li>stabilizing selection is when the two extremes of a trait have lower reproductive fitness (OWTTE) / favours average phenotype
<li>directional selection is when one extreme of the trait has lower reproductive fitness (OWTTE)
<li>disruptive/diversifying selection favours both extreme phenotypes / intermediate phenotype has lower fitness

Describe how plants transport organic compounds from where they are made to where they are stored.

","structured","SFA","
<li>(overall) process is translocation / bidirectional / movement from source to sink
<li>sugars/sucrose/organic compounds produced in leaves
<li>(loaded by) active transport / passage by apoplast route
<li>loaded into companion cells / transported in phloem / sieve tubes
<li>high concentrations of solutes at the source cause uptake of water (by osmosis)
<li>water provides hydrostatic pressure for transport (from source to sink)
<li>unloaded / stored / used at <span style=""text-decoration:underline
<li>lowers pressure at sink / creates pressure differential / water re-entry to xylem

bio2025_1241045,"C. Interaction and interdependence > C2.2. Neural signalling > C2.2.12. Effects of exogenous chemicals on synaptic transmission","

Explain the action of neonicotinoid pesticides in insects.

","structured","SFA","
• prevents synaptic/nerve transmission
• is /acts like /has similar structure to a neurotransmitter/acetylcholine
<li>(neonicotinoid pesticides) bind to acetylcholine receptors in the post synaptic membrane
<li>neonicotinoid pesticides are not broken down (as acetylcholine would be) by acetylcholinesterase/enzyme
• the receptors are overstimulated
<li>paralyses/kills the insects

bio2025_1240203,"C. Interaction and interdependence > C1.2. Cell respiration > C1.2.14. Generation of a proton gradient by flow of electrons along the electron transport chain","

Explain the role of the electron transport chain in the generation of ATP by cell respiration.

","structured","LFA","
<li>electron transport chain performs chemiosmosis / chemiosmosis generates ATP
<li>receives energy/electrons from oxidation reactions/from Krebs cycle/glycolysis

d. (two) high energy/excited electrons (from Photosystem II) are passed through an electron transport chain â
e. NADP accepts H+ (from the stroma) / electrons to produce NADPH+/reduced NADP â
f. ATP/reduced NADP/NADPH+ are used by light-independent reactions/Calvin cycle in the stroma â
g. (in Calvin cycle) carbon fixation to ribulose bisphosphate/RuBP produces G3P â

","structured","SFA","

a. neonicotinoids bind to the (acetylcholine) receptor (in insects) â

(receptors are blocked so) acetylcholine is unable to bind â
d. acetylcholinesterase/enzymes cannot break down neonicotinoids â
e. (synaptic) transmission prevented â

f. (causing) insect paralysis/death â

Explain how blood clotting occurs and the consequence for a person who has hemophilia.

","structured","LFA","

Â  Â  Â  Â Clotting process:
<ol style=""list-style-type:lower-alpha
<li>blood clotting seals cuts in the skin
<li>clotting factors are released (from platelets)
<li>thrombin is activated
<li>a cascade reaction occurs (with thrombin)
<li>(thrombin causes) fibrinogen is converted to fibrin
<li>fibrin forms a clot/blocks the cut/prevents blood from being lost
<li>if a person does not have enough clotting factors/hemophilia, the clot will not form
<li>pathogens can enter the body more easily
<li>(in hemophiliacs) blood will be lost from a cut which affects blood pressure/bleeding to death
<li>loss of blood affects amount of hemoglobin/O<sub>2</sub> carried around the body
• reference to lifestyle / menstrual/birth problems

e.g. surgery, contact sports

<ol style=""list-style-type:lower-alpha
<li>damage/cuts to blood vessels causes platelets to be activated
<li>the platelets release clotting factors

• initiates cascade of reactions
    OR
    fibrinogen is converted to fibrin
<li>forms a mesh over the damaged area
<li>prevents pathogens from entering the body
<li>phagocytes/phagocytic white blood cells in the blood travel to the site of infection
<li>(phagocytes) squeeze between the capillary cells
<li>(phagocytes) engulf/ingest/take in pathogens
<li>the pathogen is digested/broken down by/within the phagocyte
<li>lymphocytes recognize a particular fragment/antigen of a pathogen
<li>(lymphocytes) release antibodies
<li>(antibodies) provide specific immunity
<li>memory cells provide rapid response giving long-term immunity (to pathogens previously recognized)
<li>antibodies destroy pathogens

Explain the development of antibiotic resistance in terms of natural selection.

","structured","LFA","
<li>antibiotics can (generally) kill/destroy bacteria
<li>some bacteria show variation/antibiotic resistance
<li>variation/resistance is due to a random mutation

• resistant bacteria are not killed/destroyed by the antibiotic
    OR
    bacteria without the mutation die
<li>(resistant) bacteria have a selective advantage / unequal success
<li>the bacteria with this variation/resistance reproduces/multiplies
<li>mutation/gene is passed on to the offspring / the offspring will be resistant to the antibiotic
<li>resistant bacteria become more common
<li>bacteria have evolved to be resistant to the antibiotic

","structured","LFA","

<li>cell respiration produces/releases carbon dioxide
<li>glucose/carbon/organic compounds oxidised/broken down to produce/release carbon dioxide
<li>carbon dioxide released from aerobic (cell) respiration <span style=""text-decoration:underline
column2: >AND anaerobic respiration in yeast/plants (but not animals)

column1: <li>carbon dioxide released from saprotrophs/detritivores/decomposers from dead organic matter / during decay/decomposition/respiration
• (partially) decomposed organic matter can lead to the formation of peat /fossilized organic matter (coal/oil/natural gas)
<li>carbon dioxide released when carbon/organic compounds burn / during combustion (of biomass/fossil fuels) / forest fires
<li>carbon dioxide dissolves in aquatic ecosystems / can form carbonic acid/hydrogen carbonate ions
<li>reef-building corals/molluscs use calcium carbonate to make/build shells/exoskeletons or other body parts
<li>hard parts/shells/exoskeletons / precipitation of calcium carbonate to form limestone/tufa

Describe how DNA profiling can be used to establish paternity.

","structured","LFA","
<li>DNA sample is collected from the child and its (potential) parents
<li>from saliva/mouth swab/blood/other body cells
<li>PCR used to amplify/produce more copies of the DNA
<li>short tandem repeats/genes consisting of a repeating sequence of bases repeats copied/used
<li>number of repeats varies between individuals
<li>unlikely that two individuals have same number of repeats for every gene included
<li>gel electrophoresis used to separate DNA fragments according to length/number of repeats
• gel electrophoresis generates a unique pattern of bands
<li>DNA profile is the pattern of bands / diagram showing pattern of bands as in a DNA profile
<li>all bands in the childâs profile must be in one of the parentsâ profiles / OWTTE

Outline what occurs in cells in the first division of meiosis.

","structured","LFA","

a. halves the chromosome number/produces haploid cells
b. at start of meiosis each chromosome consists of two sister chromatids attached by a centromere
c. prophase (I): pairing of homologous chromosomes
d. crossing over occurs
e. chromosomes condense by supercoiling

f. metaphase (I): pairs of homologous chromosomes/bivalents move to equator of spindle
OR
metaphase (I): orientation of pairs of homologous chromosomes (prior to separation) is random

g. anaphase (I): centromeres do not divide
OR
anaphase (I): spindle fibre pulls chromosome/whole centromere with two sister chromatids to opposite poles
h. telophase (I): arrival of centromere with sister chromatids at opposite poles
Some of these can be awarded for correctly annotated diagrams.

No credit for events in meiosis II.

bio2025_2681533,"D. Continuity and Change > D3.1 Reproduction > D3.1.19. Hormonal control of pregnancy and childbirth","

Describe the hormone feedback mechanisms that help to prepare a womanâs body forÂ pregnancy, sustain the pregnancy and then give birth.

","structured","LFA","

Preparing the womanâs body for pregnancy
a. FSH stimulates estrogen secretion (by the developing follicle)
column2: b. estrogen increases FSH receptors so boosting estrogen production/so causing positive feedback
column3: c. estrogen stimulates repair/thickening of the endometrium/uterus lining
column5: > high levels of estrogen stimulate LH production/inhibit FSH secretion (negative feedback)
column6: e. LH (surge/peak) stimulates ovulation

column1: Sustaining pregnancy
f. LH stimulates the development of corpus luteum / corpus luteum secretes progesterone
column3: h. progesterone maintains lining of uterus/endometrium (for pregnancy/implant of embryo)
column5: j. HCG (secreted by embryo) stimulates maintenance of corpus luteum

column1: Childbirth
k. oxytocin stimulates uterine/myometrial contractions which stimulate oxytocin secretion
column2: l. positive feedback (mechanism used to stimulate childbirth)

Explain the reasons for none of the females in the pedigree chart having hemophilia.

","structured","SFA","
<li>hemophilia is X-linked/sex-linked/carried on the X chromosome

• females have two X chromosomes
    OR
    males only have one X chromosome
<li>hemophilia is caused by a recessive allele

• (trait) must be on both alleles to be expressed
    OR
    females would require the allele on both X chromosomes to have the disease
    OR
    females can be carriers when allele is only on one chromosome

","structured","LFA","

a. osmoregulation is regulation of water and solute/salt balance/solute concentrations
b.
column2: >nephron (is the functional unit of the kidney/osmoregulates)

column1: c. ultrafiltration in glomerulus / glomerular filtrate collected by Bowmanâs capsule

h. ADH increases permeability of collecting duct to water

j. more water reabsorbed resulting in more concentrated/hypertonic urine/less volume of urine
k. less/no ADH secreted when solute concentration (of blood) is too low/hypotonic
l. less water reabsorbed resulting in dilute/hypotonic urine/large volume of urine

bio2025_1240195,"D. Continuity and Change > D3.3 Homeostasis > D3.3.7. Role of the kidney in osmoregulation and excretion","

Explain how water balance is restored in mammals when they are dehydrated.

","structured","LFA","
<li>thirst
<li>more water drunk / more water reabsorbed from feces (in the colon/large intestine)
<li>osmoreceptors in the hypothalamus detect dehydration/high solute concentration in blood
<li>ADH secreted
<li>by the pituitary gland
<li>ADH signals to collecting duct/DCT (cells) to increase permeability to water
<li>more aquaporins (in plasma membranes of collecting duct/distal convoluted tubule cells)
<li>more water reabsorbed from filtrate (in collecting ducts/distal convoluted tubules)
<li>reabsorption by osmosis / reabsorption due to medulla being hypertonic
<li>reabsorbed water passes into the blood/reduces the solute concentration of blood
<li>smaller volume/more concentrated/hypertonic urine formed
<li>less sweating

bio2025_1240230,"D. Continuity and Change > D3.3 Homeostasis > D3.3.7. Role of the kidney in osmoregulation and excretion","

Humans need to balance water and solute concentrations and also excrete nitrogenous wastes. Explain how the different parts of the kidney carry out these processes.

","structured","LFA","
<li>humans are osmoregulators/maintain the internal concentrations of the blood/osmolarity within specific/ limited range / OWTTE
<li>glomerulus / Bowmanâs capsule (in the nephron) carry out ultrafiltration
<li>proximal convoluted tubule selectively reabsorbs glucose/solute/salts/amino acids
<li>loop of Henle maintains hypertonic conditions in the medulla/absorbs salts (by active transport)
<li>loop of Henle reabsorbs water (by osmosis)
<li>(osmoreceptors in the hypothalamus) cause production of ADH if the blood is too concentrated / person is dehydrated / OWTTE
<li>ADH causes more uptake of water/increases permeability in the collecting duct
<li>resulting in a more concentrated urine / lower volume of urine
<li>excess amino acids are broken down producing nitrogenous waste / ammonia / urea as a result
<li>ammonia is toxic and is converted into non-toxic urea
<li>urea is eliminated in the urine

Marks can be awarded to clearly annotated diagrams.

","structured","SFA","

bio2025_1240239,"D. Continuity and Change > D4.1 Natural selection > D4.1.1. Natural selection as the mechanism driving evolutionary change","

Explain how natural selection can cause traits such as drought resistance to develop in wild plants.

","structured","LFA","
<li>(natural selection occurs if) there is variation in degree of drought resistance among members of a population/same species
<li>variation is caused by mutations (when changes occur in the DNA/nucleic bases/chromosomes)
<li>variation during meiosis occurs (with separation of chromosomes)
<li>variation occurs during sexual reproduction (as different alleles combine)
<li>some variations make some plants more drought-resistant
<li>example of variations: deeper roots/more storage tissue for water/thicker cuticles/less opening of stomata/other verifiable variations

• these variations let some survive and reproduce better/have more offspring
    OR
    (these variations) confer selective advantage
<li>these variations/characteristics are passed onto offspring which survive better
<li>natural selection increases the frequency of these characteristics
<li>eventually leads to changes/evolution in the species / more drought-resistant plants
